title: Microscopic Origins of Collapse Models: Decoherence from Graviton Bremsstrahlung
abstract: Some collapse models proposed that gravitational effects cause the instability of mass distribution superpositions, leading to wave function collapse. In this paper, we utilize the quantum Boltzmann equation (QBE) to analyze the behavior of a fermion in a spatial superposition under graviton emission. We introduce a quantitative measure that links the stability of the superposition to the spatial separation, particle mass, and gravitational coupling. By examining the collision term in the QBE, we derive the decoherence rate and show how it depends on these parameters. Our results provide a detailed framework for understanding gravity induced decoherence, bridging the gap between quantum field theory and collapse models. We also discuss the implications of these findings for experimental tests of gravitationally induced wave function collapse and the broader class of collapse models known as dissipative continuous spontaneous localization (CSL) model.
summary: This paper proposes that the emission of gravitons (Bremsstrahlung) during particle motion provides a microscopic origin for wave function collapse. It demonstrates that while individual gravitational interactions are weak, the collective effect in large masses leads to rapid decoherence and the emergence of classical behavior.
main_problem: Investigating whether the wave function collapse described by phenomenological models (like CSL or Diósi-Penrose) can be derived from a microscopic quantum field-theoretic framework, specifically through graviton-induced decoherence.
main_result: The paper derives a decoherence rate from graviton Bremsstrahlung that scales quadratically with the number of constituents and mass, providing a mechanism for the emergence of classicality in macroscopic systems.
method: The author utilizes the Quantum Boltzmann Equation (QBE) and the S-matrix to analyze the interaction between a fermion and a graviton bath, applying the Born-Markov and weak-field approximations.
model_or_system: A spin-1/2 fermionic spinor field in a spatial superposition interacting with soft graviton degrees of freedom (tensor field) via a graviton Bremsstrahlung process.
key_observables: The decay of the off-diagonal coherence element (rho_12) of the density matrix and the decoherence rate (Gamma).
important_parameters: Spatial separation (delta x), particle mass (m_f), gravitational coupling (kappa), number of constituents (N), and the graviton spectral density.
main_assumptions: Weak-field limit of General Relativity, Born-Markov approximation, non-relativistic regime for the fermion, and an isotropic, unpolarized graviton bath.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the graviton Bremsstrahlung process for a fermion in superposition; Figure 2 is a contour plot showing decoherence time as a function of total mass and number of constituents.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the field operators and interaction Hamiltonian, progresses to constructing the QBE collision term, derives the decoherence rate and scaling laws, and concludes by discussing implications for collapse models and experimental tests.
why_interesting: It provides a fundamental link between quantum field theory and the emergence of classicality, offering a microscopic derivation for the decoherence-induced suppression of superpositions that is central to the study of open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Graviton Bremsstrahlung process from a fermion in a spatial superposition state.

FIG. 2. Contour plot of the gravitationally induced decoherence time τdec = Γ−1 as a function of the total mass M and the number of constituents N. The decoherence rate scales as Γ ∝N 2, reflecting the coherent enhancement of graviton emission in composite systems, while finite-size effects introduce an exponential suppression through the ratio of the object size to the graviton coherence length. The dashed vertical line indicates the electron mass, highlighting the regime where single particle quantum coherence is preserved.
